title: Early Britain
overview:
Britain has been inhabited for thousands of years, with each wave of settlers and invaders leaving a lasting legacy. From Stone Age hunter-gatherers and Bronze Age farmers to Iron Age Celtic people, Romans, Anglo-Saxons, Vikings and finally the Normans in 1066 - each group shaped the language, culture, laws and identity of Britain as we know it today.

This lesson covers the history of Britain from the Stone Age through to the Norman Conquest of 1066.

study_sections.heading: Stone Age Britain
study_sections.content:
The first people to live in Britain were hunter-gatherers, in what we call the Stone Age. For much of the Stone Age, Britain was connected to the continent by a land bridge. People came and went, following the herds of deer and horses which they hunted. Britain only became permanently separated from the continent by the Channel about 10,000 years ago.

The first farmers arrived in Britain about 6,000 years ago. The ancestors of these first farmers probably came from south-east Europe. These people built houses, tombs and monuments on the land.

One of these monuments, Stonehenge, still stands in what is now the English county of Wiltshire. Stonehenge was probably a special gathering place for seasonal ceremonies.

Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe. It has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age.

study_sections.heading: Bronze Age and Iron Age
study_sections.content:
Around 4,000 years ago, people learned to make bronze. We call this period the Bronze Age. People lived in roundhouses and buried their dead in tombs called round barrows. The people of the Bronze Age were accomplished metalworkers who made many beautiful objects in bronze and gold, including tools, ornaments and weapons.

The Bronze Age was followed by the Iron Age, when people learned how to make weapons and tools out of iron. People still lived in roundhouses, grouped together into larger settlements, and sometimes defended sites called hill forts. A very impressive hill fort can still be seen today at Maiden Castle, in the English county of Dorset.

Most people were farmers, craft workers or warriors. The language they spoke was part of the Celtic language family. Similar languages were spoken across Europe in the Iron Age, and related languages are still spoken today in some parts of Wales, Scotland and Ireland.

The people of the Iron Age had a sophisticated culture and economy. They made the first coins to be minted in Britain, some inscribed with the names of Iron Age kings. This marks the beginnings of British history.

study_sections.heading: The Romans
study_sections.content:
Julius Caesar led a Roman invasion of Britain in 55 BC. This was unsuccessful and for nearly 100 years Britain remained separate from the Roman Empire.

In AD 43 the Emperor Claudius led the Roman army in a new invasion. This time, there was resistance from some of the British tribes but the Romans were successful in occupying almost all of Britain. One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England. She is still remembered today and there is a statue of her on Westminster Bridge in London, near the Houses of Parliament.

Areas of what is now Scotland were never conquered by the Romans. The Emperor Hadrian built a wall in the north of England to keep out the Picts (ancestors of the Scottish people). Parts of Hadrian's Wall, including the forts of Housesteads and Vindolanda, can still be seen. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

The Romans remained in Britain for 400 years. They built roads and public buildings, created a structure of law, and introduced new plants and animals. It was during the 3rd and 4th centuries AD that the first Christian communities began to appear in Britain.

The Roman army left Britain in AD 410 to defend other parts of the Roman Empire and never returned.

study_sections.heading: The Anglo-Saxons
study_sections.content:
After the Romans left in AD 410, Britain was invaded by tribes from northern Europe: the Jutes, the Angles and the Saxons. The languages they spoke are the basis of modern-day English. By about AD 600, Anglo-Saxon kingdoms were established in Britain, mainly in what is now England.

The burial place of one Anglo-Saxon king was at Sutton Hoo in modern Suffolk. This king was buried with treasure and armour, placed in a ship which was then covered by a mound of earth.

The Anglo-Saxons were not Christians when they first came to Britain. Missionaries came to Britain to preach about Christianity. Missionaries from Ireland spread the religion in the north. The most famous were St Patrick, who became the patron saint of Ireland, and St Columba, who founded a monastery on the island of Iona, off the coast of what is now Scotland. St Augustine led missionaries from Rome who spread Christianity in the south. St Augustine became the first Archbishop of Canterbury.

study_sections.heading: The Vikings
study_sections.content:
The Vikings came from Denmark and Norway. They first visited Britain in AD 789 to raid coastal towns and take away goods and slaves. Then they began to stay and form their own communities in the east of England and Scotland.

The Anglo-Saxon kingdoms in England united under King Alfred the Great, who defeated the Vikings. Many Viking invaders stayed in Britain - especially in the east and north of England, in an area known as the Danelaw. Many place names in this area, such as Grimsby and Scunthorpe, come from Viking languages.

Anglo-Saxon kings continued to rule what is now England, except for a short period when there were Danish kings. The first of these was Cnut (also called Canute).

In the north, the threat of attack by Vikings encouraged the people to unite under one king, Kenneth MacAlpin. The term Scotland began to be used to describe that country.

study_sections.heading: The Norman Conquest 1066
study_sections.content:
In 1066, an invasion led by William, the Duke of Normandy (in what is now northern France), defeated Harold, the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings. Harold was killed in the battle. William became king of England and is known as William the Conqueror.

The battle is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, known as the Bayeux Tapestry, which can still be seen in France today.

The Norman Conquest was the last successful foreign invasion of England and led to many changes in government and social structures. Norman French, the language of the new ruling class, influenced the development of the English language as we know it today. Initially the Normans also conquered Wales, but the Welsh gradually won territory back. The Normans took over some land on the border with Scotland but did not invade Scotland.

William sent people all over England to draw up lists of all the towns and villages, their people, land owners and animals. This was called the Domesday Book. It still exists today and gives a picture of society in England just after the Norman Conquest.

key_facts: The first people in Britain were Stone Age hunter-gatherers.
Britain became permanently separated from the continent about 10,000 years ago.
The first farmers arrived in Britain about 6,000 years ago.
Stonehenge is in Wiltshire and was probably a gathering place for seasonal ceremonies.
Skara Brae on Orkney is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe.
The Bronze Age began about 4,000 years ago.
Bronze Age people lived in roundhouses and buried their dead in round barrows.
The Iron Age followed the Bronze Age.
Maiden Castle in Dorset is an impressive Iron Age hill fort.
Iron Age people spoke Celtic languages, still spoken in parts of Wales, Scotland and Ireland.
Iron Age people made the first coins to be minted in Britain.
Julius Caesar led a Roman invasion in 55 BC, which was unsuccessful.
Emperor Claudius led the successful Roman invasion in AD 43.
Boudicca was the queen of the Iceni who fought against the Romans.
There is a statue of Boudicca on Westminster Bridge in London.
Hadrian's Wall was built to keep out the Picts.
Housesteads and Vindolanda are Roman forts on Hadrian's Wall.
Hadrian's Wall is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
The Romans stayed in Britain for 400 years.
The first Christian communities appeared in Britain in the 3rd and 4th centuries AD.
The Roman army left Britain in AD 410.
After the Romans left, the Jutes, Angles and Saxons invaded from northern Europe.
Anglo-Saxon languages are the basis of modern English.
By about AD 600, Anglo-Saxon kingdoms were established.
Sutton Hoo in Suffolk was the burial place of an Anglo-Saxon king.
St Patrick became the patron saint of Ireland.
St Columba founded a monastery on the island of Iona.
St Augustine became the first Archbishop of Canterbury.
The Vikings came from Denmark and Norway.
Vikings first visited Britain in AD 789.
King Alfred the Great defeated the Vikings.
The Danelaw was the area of Viking settlement in east and north England.
Cnut (Canute) was the first Danish king of England.
Kenneth MacAlpin united Scotland against the Vikings.
The Battle of Hastings was in 1066.
William, Duke of Normandy, defeated Harold at the Battle of Hastings.
Harold was killed at the Battle of Hastings.
William became known as William the Conqueror.
The Bayeux Tapestry commemorates the Battle of Hastings.
The Norman Conquest was the last successful foreign invasion of England.
The Domesday Book was a record of towns, villages, landowners and animals in England.
memory_hook: Remember early Britain with 'Stone, Bronze, Iron, Romans, Saxons, Vikings, Normans'. Key dates: 55 BC (Caesar - failed), AD 43 (Claudius - succeeded), AD 410 (Romans left), AD 789 (Vikings first raid), 1066 (Norman Conquest). Think 'Caesar Failed, Claudius Conquered, Saxons Settled, Vikings Raided, William Won'.

questions.prompt: What were the first inhabitants of Britain?
questions.explanation: The first people to live in Britain were hunter-gatherers in the Stone Age.

questions.prompt: How long ago did Britain become separated from the continent?
questions.explanation: Britain only became permanently separated from the continent by the Channel about 10,000 years ago.

questions.prompt: When did the first farmers arrive in Britain?
questions.explanation: The first farmers arrived in Britain about 6,000 years ago from south-east Europe.

questions.prompt: Where is Stonehenge?
questions.explanation: Stonehenge still stands in what is now the English county of Wiltshire.

questions.prompt: What is Skara Brae?
options: The best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe, on Orkney
questions.explanation: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe.

questions.prompt: When did the Bronze Age begin?
questions.explanation: Around 4,000 years ago, people learned to make bronze, beginning the Bronze Age.

questions.prompt: What type of tombs did Bronze Age people use?
questions.explanation: Bronze Age people buried their dead in tombs called round barrows.

questions.prompt: Where is Maiden Castle?
questions.explanation: A very impressive Iron Age hill fort can still be seen at Maiden Castle in Dorset.

questions.prompt: What language did Iron Age people speak?
questions.explanation: Iron Age people spoke Celtic languages, related to those still spoken in Wales, Scotland and Ireland.

questions.prompt: When did Julius Caesar invade Britain?
questions.explanation: Julius Caesar led a Roman invasion of Britain in 55 BC, but it was unsuccessful.

questions.prompt: When did the successful Roman invasion occur?
questions.explanation: In AD 43, Emperor Claudius led the successful Roman invasion.

questions.prompt: Who was Boudicca?
options: Queen of the Iceni who fought against the Romans
questions.explanation: Boudicca was the queen of the Iceni in eastern England who fought against the Romans.

questions.prompt: Where is there a statue of Boudicca?
questions.explanation: There is a statue of Boudicca on Westminster Bridge in London, near the Houses of Parliament.

questions.prompt: Why was Hadrian's Wall built?
options: To mark the border with Wales
questions.explanation: Emperor Hadrian built a wall in the north of England to keep out the Picts.

questions.prompt: How long did the Romans stay in Britain?
questions.explanation: The Romans remained in Britain for 400 years.

questions.prompt: When did the Romans leave Britain?
questions.explanation: The Roman army left Britain in AD 410 to defend other parts of the Roman Empire.

questions.prompt: Which tribes invaded after the Romans left?
questions.explanation: After the Romans left, Britain was invaded by the Jutes, the Angles and the Saxons from northern Europe.

questions.prompt: What are Anglo-Saxon languages the basis of?
questions.explanation: The languages of the Jutes, Angles and Saxons are the basis of modern-day English.

questions.prompt: Where was the Anglo-Saxon royal burial at Sutton Hoo?
questions.explanation: The burial place of an Anglo-Saxon king was at Sutton Hoo in modern Suffolk.

questions.prompt: Who became the patron saint of Ireland?
questions.explanation: St Patrick became the patron saint of Ireland.

questions.prompt: Who founded a monastery on the island of Iona?
questions.explanation: St Columba founded a monastery on the island of Iona, off the coast of what is now Scotland.

questions.prompt: Who became the first Archbishop of Canterbury?
questions.explanation: St Augustine, who led missionaries from Rome, became the first Archbishop of Canterbury.

questions.prompt: Where did the Vikings come from?
questions.explanation: The Vikings came from Denmark and Norway.

questions.prompt: When did Vikings first visit Britain?
questions.explanation: The Vikings first visited Britain in AD 789 to raid coastal towns.

questions.prompt: Who defeated the Vikings in England?
questions.explanation: The Anglo-Saxon kingdoms united under King Alfred the Great, who defeated the Vikings.

questions.prompt: What was the Danelaw?
options: The area of Viking settlement in east and north England
questions.explanation: The Danelaw was the area of Viking settlement in east and north England.

questions.prompt: Who was the first Danish king of England?
questions.explanation: The first Danish king of England was Cnut, also called Canute.

questions.prompt: Who united Scotland against the Vikings?
questions.explanation: In the north, the threat of Viking attacks encouraged the people to unite under Kenneth MacAlpin.

questions.prompt: When was the Battle of Hastings?
questions.explanation: In 1066, William Duke of Normandy defeated Harold at the Battle of Hastings.

questions.prompt: Who defeated Harold at the Battle of Hastings?
questions.explanation: William, the Duke of Normandy, defeated Harold at Hastings and became William the Conqueror.

questions.prompt: What commemorates the Battle of Hastings?
questions.explanation: The Bayeux Tapestry, still in France, commemorates the Battle of Hastings.

questions.prompt: What was the Domesday Book?
options: A record of towns, villages, landowners and animals in England
A history of the Norman Conquest
questions.explanation: William sent people all over England to compile the Domesday Book, a survey of land and ownership.

questions.prompt: Was the Norman Conquest the last successful foreign invasion of England?
options: No, there have been others since
It was not a foreign invasion
questions.explanation: The Norman Conquest of 1066 was the last successful foreign invasion of England.

supportive_messages.high_score: Brilliant! You know early British history all the way back to the Stone Age! 🏛️
supportive_messages.medium_score: Good work! Focus on the key dates and the order of invaders! 📚
supportive_messages.low_score: Early history is complex - keep reviewing the timeline of peoples and invasions! 💪
